sourceMovie.title: Shazam! Fury of the Gods
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Shazam! Fury of the Gods works as a comedy movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The comedy and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Shazam!
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The comedy energy of Shazam! Fury of the Gods is alive in Shazam! (2019), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a funny payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Nimona
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Nimona (2023) operates in the same comedy as Shazam! Fury of the Gods, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness (2022) belongs to the same conversation as Shazam! Fury of the Gods — both anchor their action in fantasy emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Despicable Me 4
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Shazam! Fury of the Gods's comedy stayed with you, Despicable Me 4 (2024) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The action through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Hotel Transylvania: Transformania
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Shazam! Fury of the Gods excels in comedy, Hotel Transylvania: Transformania (2022) doubles down on fantasy to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Teen Titans Go! To the Movies
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Teen Titans Go! To the Movies (2018) is the natural next step after Shazam! Fury of the Gods: same comedy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and action elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Justice League: Gods and Monsters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Shazam! Fury of the Gods who respond to its action will find Justice League: Gods and Monsters (2015) immediately familiar. Both films share a fantasy sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Shazam! Fury of the Gods fans keep landing on Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse (2018) — the action is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Captain Marvel
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Captain Marvel draws from the same action well as Shazam! Fury of the Gods, channeling adventure in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2019 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: 10 Things I Hate About You
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: 10 Things I Hate About You (1999) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that Shazam! Fury of the Gods later expanded. If Shazam! Fury of the Gods resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

faq.question: What should I watch after Shazam! Fury of the Gods?
faq.answer: Try Shazam!, Nimona, Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness—each shares DNA with Shazam! Fury of the Gods while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Shazam! Fury of the Gods?
faq.answer: Shazam! Fury of the Gods (2023) is best described as Comedy, Action, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Shazam! Fury of the Gods part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Shazam! Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
